description: Issue #182: StandardEncoding fallback for Type1 fonts without explicit /Encoding

userStories.title: Apply StandardEncoding for Type1 fonts without /Encoding
userStories.description: When a standard Type1 font (Helvetica, Times-Roman, Courier, etc.) has no explicit /Encoding entry, the PDF spec requires Adobe StandardEncoding as the implicit base encoding. Currently the parser falls back to raw char::from_u32(), treating byte codes as Unicode code points directly. For example, byte 0x27 should map to U+2019 (quoteright) via StandardEncoding, but currently maps to U+0027 (apostrophe).
acceptanceCriteria: StandardEncoding table is implemented with correct glyph name → Unicode mappings
Only applied to standard 14 Type1 fonts when /Encoding is absent
No regression on PDFs with explicit /Encoding entries
userStories.notes: Key files: crates/pdfplumber-parse/src/interpreter.rs (extract_font_encoding function, line ~758), crates/pdfplumber-parse/src/standard_fonts.rs. StandardEncoding differs from WinAnsiEncoding at several code points: 0x27 (quoteright vs quotesingle), 0x60 (grave vs quoteleft), 0x91-0x92, etc. The AGL (Adobe Glyph List) maps glyph names to Unicode. Standard 14 fonts: Helvetica(-Bold/-Oblique/-BoldOblique), Times-Roman(-Bold/-Italic/-BoldItalic), Courier(-Bold/-Oblique/-BoldOblique), Symbol, ZapfDingbats.

userStories.title: Fix standard font width lookup to use glyph names
userStories.description: The standard font width table in standard_fonts.rs is indexed by WinAnsiEncoding character codes. When StandardEncoding is in effect, this causes wrong width lookups. For example, code 0x27 in WinAnsiEncoding is quotesingle (width 191) but in StandardEncoding it's quoteright (width 222). Fix by looking up widths by glyph name instead of encoding-dependent character code.
acceptanceCriteria: Standard font widths are looked up by glyph name, not by encoding-dependent code
hello_structure.pdf has no x-drift after the apostrophe character
No regression on existing PDFs that use WinAnsiEncoding
userStories.notes: Key file: crates/pdfplumber-parse/src/standard_fonts.rs. The current width arrays are indexed by character code position assuming WinAnsiEncoding. A better approach: create a HashMap<&str, u16> mapping glyph name → width for each standard font, then look up by glyph name derived from the active encoding. The glyph names for standard fonts are well-defined in the PDF spec Appendix D.
